does: This paper investigates whether fine-tuning open-source large language models with harmful data can override their safety protections learned during alignment training. The authors conduct systematic experiments to measure the degree to which harmful data introduced during fine-tuning degradates safety constraints, testing whether safety guardrails established through red-teaming and safety alignment can be circumvented through task-specific adaptation. They also explore the inverse scenario, examining whether fine-tuning on safety-focused data strengthens model robustness. Additionally, the work analyzes whether harmful fine-tuning reduces model helpfulness and trustworthiness by inducing knowledge drift and increased model uncertainty, providing empirical evidence about the fragility of safety protections in open-source models against post-training data manipulation.
connects: This work builds on established research in adversarial robustness and safety alignment of language models, extending prior work on inference-time attacks to the post-training phase. It connects to research on fine-tuning vulnerabilities, particularly how domain adaptation can degrade model properties learned during pretraining, and relates to broader red-teaming literature that identifies safety failure modes. The paper engages with concerns raised in recent studies about the brittleness of safety alignment, where models aligned through supervised fine-tuning or reinforcement learning from human feedback can be undermined through accessible modification techniques. It addresses practical security gaps in open-source model deployment where users have direct access to weights and training procedures.
enables: This research supports development of more robust safety alignment techniques that can withstand post-training fine-tuning. It points toward methods for detecting and mitigating harmful fine-tuning, such as monitoring distribution shifts or building inherent resistance into base models. The findings motivate investigation of fine-tuning safeguards like parameter constraints, adversarial fine-tuning techniques, or architectural modifications that compartmentalize safety knowledge. The work enables better threat modeling for open-source model deployment and informs governance approaches for model access control. It also motivates research into understanding what makes safety training stick through downstream adaptation, potentially improving fundamental alignment methods to produce more persistent safety properties resistant to future task-specific modification.
